county_metadata.record_losses: No major record losses. Ormsby County records were transferred to Carson City upon consolidation in 1969.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Carson City became a consolidated municipality (independent city) in 1969 when Ormsby County was dissolved. It is the only independent city in Nevada. As state capital since 1861, it houses the Nevada State Library, Archives and Public Records. Pre-1969 records filed under Ormsby County. Named after Kit Carson.
county_metadata.research_tips: Carson City absorbed Ormsby County in 1969, so researchers must check records under both names — Ormsby County for anything before the merger and Carson City afterward. The Nevada State Library, Archives and Public Records is physically located here and holds territorial-era records from 1861, governor's papers, and state agency records that supplement local research. The Carson City Recorder's office has deeds from the Ormsby County era. The Nevada State Museum (former U.S. Mint building) holds mining assay records and Carson City Mint coin production logs that can identify mint workers. FamilySearch has microfilmed Ormsby County records including early marriage records from the 1860s. The Stewart Indian School (1890–1980) operated here and its student records, now at NARA-San Bruno, document Native American families from across Nevada and neighboring states.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Deed records from Ormsby County era through present; online index available

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records continuing from Ormsby County. Grantor/grantee indexes available.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete — Ormsby County records transferred intact in 1969.

source_profiles.source_name: Carson City / Ormsby County Marriage Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Ormsby County marriage records from territorial era. Post-1969 filed under Carson City.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — early territorial records survive. FamilySearch has microfilm of Ormsby County marriages.

source_profiles.source_name: Federal Census — Ormsby County / Carson City
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census 1870–1950 (1890 destroyed). Enumerated as Ormsby County through 1960. Nevada Territory 1860 census also exists.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete for surviving census years. 1860 territorial census at Nevada State Archives.

source_profiles.source_name: Nevada Death Records — Carson City / Ormsby County
source_profiles.coverage_notes: County-recorded deaths before state registration (1911). FamilySearch has indexed Nevada death records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial — pre-1911 county death recording was inconsistent across Nevada.

source_profiles.repository: NARA San Bruno / Nevada State Museum
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Enrollment records, student files, and photographs from Stewart Indian School, which served Paiute, Shoshone, and Washoe students from across Nevada and neighboring states.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — NARA San Bruno holds the bulk of student records. Some records also at Nevada Indian Commission.

source_profiles.source_name: Nevada Appeal and Carson City Newspapers
source_profiles.coverage_notes: The Nevada Appeal (founded 1865) and other Carson City papers. Legal notices, obituaries, legislative news.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial digitization; microfilm at Nevada State Library for extended coverage.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major fire losses. Some early territorial records are sparse due to low population in the 1860s–1870s.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of Nevada's original nine counties created in 1861 when Nevada Territory was organized. Named for Fort Churchill, a military post established in 1860 after the Pyramid Lake War. County seat moved from Buckland to Fallon in 1904 after the Newlands Reclamation Project brought irrigation agriculture. Stillwater Range and Carson Sink define the landscape.
county_metadata.research_tips: Churchill County's genealogical landscape splits into two eras: the sparse military outpost/overland trail period (1860s–1900) and the post-Newlands Reclamation Act agricultural boom after 1902. Fort Churchill military records (1860–1869) are at NARA and document soldiers and camp followers during the Civil War era. The Newlands Project brought a wave of homesteaders after 1902, and the Bureau of Reclamation records at NARA-Denver document water rights allotments that effectively function as land grants. The Churchill County Museum in Fallon has an excellent local history collection including oral histories and pioneer family files. The county recorder has deeds from the 1860s. Fallon Naval Air Station (established 1942) generated military personnel records. The Stillwater National Wildlife Refuge area overlaps with traditional Paiute territory — check Fallon Paiute-Shoshone Tribe records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County recorder deed index; some records online, most require in-person access

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from territorial era. Includes homestead patents from Newlands Reclamation Project era (1902+).
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete — no fire losses.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Post returns, muster rolls, and personnel records from Fort Churchill during the Civil War era. Documents soldiers stationed to protect the overland mail route.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — NARA holds the complete post records.

source_profiles.source_name: Newlands Reclamation Project Water Rights and Homestead Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Water right allotments and homestead applications from the Newlands Reclamation Project, which transformed Churchill County into irrigated farmland.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well preserved at NARA Denver. Bureau of Reclamation also holds duplicate records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: County clerk marriage licenses and certificates. FamilySearch has microfilmed early records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — continuous from territorial era.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census 1870–1950 (1890 destroyed). Population was very small before 1902 reclamation project.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete for surviving years. Note dramatic population increase after 1902.

source_profiles.source_name: Churchill County Eagle and Fallon Standard
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Fallon-area newspapers from the agricultural boom era. Legal notices, homestead announcements, obituaries.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial digitization; microfilm at Nevada State Library.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major record losses. Pre-1909 records are in Lincoln County (Pioche).
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed from Lincoln County in 1909. Named for William Andrews Clark, Montana senator who built the San Pedro, Los Angeles & Salt Lake Railroad through the area. Las Vegas was founded as a railroad town in 1905. Hoover Dam (1931–1936) and post-WWII gaming/entertainment drove explosive growth. Now contains over 70% of Nevada's population.
county_metadata.research_tips: Clark County is Nevada's most populous jurisdiction but genealogically young — most records date from 1905 onward. Pre-1909 research requires checking Lincoln County (Pioche) records. The Clark County Recorder's Office has deeds from 1909 and offers online searching. Las Vegas was essentially a railroad company town from 1905–1930; check Union Pacific Railroad records at the Utah State Archives and UP Historical Museum in Omaha. Hoover Dam construction (1931–1936) brought thousands of workers — Six Companies employment records are at NARA and the Boulder City Museum. The UNLV Special Collections holds extensive Las Vegas history materials including gaming industry records, hotel worker records, and Henderson industrial plant files. Marriage records are abundant — Clark County became nationally famous for quick marriages and the 'wedding capital of the world.' Check the Clark County Marriage Bureau for records from 1909 onward.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Online index and document images from 1985; earlier records require in-person access

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Land records from county formation. Online images from 1985; earlier records on-site. Pre-1909 records in Lincoln County.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete — no losses.

source_profiles.repository: Clark County Clerk / Nevada State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Clark County is the 'Wedding Capital of the World.' Millions of marriage licenses issued. Records from 1909 onward.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent — Clark County marriage records are voluminous and well maintained.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: First enumerated as Clark County in 1910. Pre-1909 area in Lincoln County census. Population: 3,321 in 1910; massive growth post-WWII.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete for available census years.

source_profiles.source_name: Nellis Air Force Base and WWII-era Military Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Las Vegas Army Air Field (later Nellis AFB) personnel records, Basic Magnesium Inc. worker files from Henderson, and defense industry employment records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — UNLV has collected significant WWII-era Clark County materials.

source_profiles.source_name: Clark County District Court — Divorce Records
source_profiles.repository: Clark County District Court, Las Vegas
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Divorce records from county formation. Las Vegas became a divorce destination after Reno, especially mid-20th century.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete and well maintained.

source_profiles.repository: Clark County District Court, Las Vegas
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, estates, guardianships from 1909. Pre-1909 probate in Lincoln County.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete — no losses.

source_profiles.source_name: Las Vegas Age and Las Vegas Review-Journal
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Las Vegas newspapers from founding era. The Las Vegas Age (1905+) is the earliest. Legal notices, obituaries, construction news.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good digitization coverage through UNLV and Chronicling America.

county_metadata.record_losses: No significant record losses. Early records well preserved at county offices.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of Nevada's original nine counties created in 1861. Named for Stephen A. Douglas. Genoa (originally Mormon Station, 1851) is the oldest non-native settlement in Nevada. County seat moved from Genoa to Minden in 1916. Includes western shore of Lake Tahoe. Carson Valley was the first area of white settlement in what became Nevada.
county_metadata.research_tips: Douglas County contains Genoa, Nevada's oldest settlement (Mormon Station, founded 1851), making it crucial for tracing the earliest non-native families in the state. The Mormon Station era (1851–1857) is documented in LDS Church records at the Church History Library in Salt Lake City — check Brigham Young's correspondence and early Carson Valley Ward records. The Douglas County Recorder in Minden has deeds from the territorial era. County seat was Genoa until 1916, so early court and probate records were filed there. The Douglas County Historical Society in Gardnerville maintains family files and pioneer histories. Lake Tahoe resort development from the 1860s generated hotel and timber company records. The Dangberg family ranch records (a major Carson Valley operation) are at the Nevada Historical Society in Reno and document ranching families and Chinese laborers. Swiss-Italian and German immigrant families settled the Carson Valley for dairy farming — check immigration/naturalization records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County recorder deed index; online access to recent records, early records in-person

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Land records from territorial era. Includes some of Nevada's earliest recorded deeds from the Carson Valley.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete — well preserved.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from territorial era. Some of Nevada's earliest marriage records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — continuous from early settlement era.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: 1860 territorial census includes Carson Valley (pre-county). Federal census 1870–1950 (1890 destroyed).
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete for surviving years. 1860 territorial census is at Nevada State Archives.

source_profiles.source_name: Mormon Station / Carson Valley Ward LDS Records
source_profiles.repository: Church History Library, Salt Lake City
source_profiles.coverage_notes: LDS ward records from the Mormon Station era — the earliest organized religious community in Nevada. Membership, blessings, correspondence.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — LDS Church archives preserve early ward records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, estates, and guardianships. Filed at Genoa courthouse until 1916, then Minden.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well preserved from territorial era.

source_profiles.source_name: Douglas County Newspapers — Genoa and Gardnerville
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Territorial and early statehood newspapers from the Carson Valley. Legal notices, ranching news, obituaries.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial — some early issues lost. Best coverage from 1890s onward.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major fire losses. County records are well maintained since formation.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed from Lander County in 1869. Named for the city of Elko, a Central Pacific Railroad town founded in 1868. Nevada's largest county by area (17,203 sq mi). Home to significant Basque, Chinese, and Shoshone communities. Includes the Ruby Mountains and vast ranching country.
county_metadata.research_tips: Elko County is essential for researching Basque immigrant families — the largest Basque community outside Europe settled here for sheep ranching, and the Basque Museum in Boise plus the Center for Basque Studies at UNR hold family histories, boarding house records, and community organization files. Chinese railroad workers and miners left traces in Elko Chinatown records, Chinese Exclusion Act case files (NARA San Bruno), and mining company payrolls. The Central Pacific Railroad built Elko as a division point in 1868 — check Southern Pacific Railroad records at the California State Railroad Museum and NARA. Elko County Recorder has deeds from 1869. The Northeastern Nevada Museum in Elko holds significant local genealogical collections including ranching family papers. Shoshone tribal records from the Te-Moak Bands are important for Native American research. Mining claims in the Bull Run, Tuscarora, and other mining districts are filed at the county recorder.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County recorder land records; index available, images may require in-person access

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deeds and mining claims from county formation. Includes extensive mining claim records from Tuscarora, Bull Run, and other districts.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete — no losses.

source_profiles.source_name: Chinese Exclusion Act Case Files — Elko District
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Case files from Chinese Exclusion Act enforcement in northeastern Nevada. Documents Chinese miners, railroad workers, and merchants in Elko and surrounding mining camps.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — NARA San Bruno holds extensive Chinese exclusion case files for Nevada.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from county formation. Includes Basque, Chinese, and Shoshone marriages.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — continuous from 1869.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census 1870–1950 (1890 destroyed). Note large Chinese population in 1870–1880 censuses and Basque listings from 1900+.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete for surviving census years.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Bureau of Indian Affairs records for the Western Shoshone bands in northeastern Nevada. Census rolls, allotment records, and agency correspondence.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — NARA holds BIA records. Some tribal records restricted.

source_profiles.source_name: Elko Independent and Elko Free Press
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Northeastern Nevada newspapers with railroad, mining, and ranching news. Basque community notices. Obituaries and legal ads.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial digitization; microfilm at Nevada State Library and UNR.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major fire losses at the county level, though individual mining camp records were lost in camp fires at Aurora and Goldfield.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of Nevada's original nine counties created in 1861. Originally included Aurora (first county seat, disputed with California until 1863 boundary survey placed it in Nevada). Goldfield boom (1902–1910) made it briefly one of Nevada's most populous areas. County seat moved to Hawthorne (1883), then to Goldfield (1907). Mineral County carved out in 1911, taking Hawthorne.
county_metadata.research_tips: Esmeralda County is a classic Nevada mining county with boom-and-bust cycles that scattered records. Aurora (1860s county seat) was a silver boomtown on the California-Nevada border — the boundary dispute means some Aurora records may be in Mono County, California, as well as in Nevada state archives. Goldfield's spectacular gold rush (1902–1910) drew thousands, and mine employment records from the Goldfield Consolidated Mining Company are at the Nevada Historical Society in Reno. The county recorder in Goldfield has mining claims and deeds from the territorial era. Goldfield had a significant labor history (1907 IWW strike) and union records at UNR Special Collections document mining families. The area around Tonopah (now Nye County) and Goldfield shared populations — check both counties. Ghost towns like Rhyolite, Bullfrog, and Pioneer have scattered records. FamilySearch has microfilmed Esmeralda County vital records from the 1860s.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: No online access; county recorder in Goldfield handles in-person requests

source_profiles.source_name: Esmeralda County Recorder — Land and Mining Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deeds and mining claims from territorial era. Massive volume of mining claims from the Goldfield boom (1902–1910).
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete at county level; some individual mining camp records lost.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from territorial era through mid-20th century. Includes records from the Goldfield boom era.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — early records survive from Aurora era.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census 1870–1950 (1890 destroyed). Note large population in 1870 (Aurora boom) and 1910 (Goldfield boom), with very small population between.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete for surviving years.

source_profiles.repository: Nevada State Archives / Esmeralda County Courthouse, Goldfield
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Civil and criminal case files including mining disputes, claim-jumping cases, and the 1907 Goldfield labor strike proceedings.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — older records transferred to state archives.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Goldfield boom-era newspapers with mining claim notices, labor news, obituaries, and legal advertisements.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial — some issues lost. Best coverage 1904–1912.

county_metadata.record_losses: Town of Eureka suffered fires in 1879 and 1880 that destroyed portions of the business district, but county courthouse records survived.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed from Lander County in 1873 during the silver-lead smelting boom. Named for the town of Eureka, which became one of Nevada's major smelting centers. The Eureka and Palisade Railroad connected the mines to the Central Pacific. Italian and Cornish miners were significant immigrant groups. Population peaked around 7,000 in the 1870s–1880s before declining.
county_metadata.research_tips: Eureka County is a quintessential Nevada mining county where smelting operations drove a concentrated boom from 1869–1890. The Eureka Sentinel newspaper (1870–1897) is extensively digitized and is one of Nevada's best historical newspapers for genealogical notices. Italian charcoal burners (carbonari) were a distinctive workforce here — the 1879 'Charcoal Burners' War' generated court records and newspaper coverage that document these families. Cornish miners brought hard-rock mining expertise and left traces in mining company payrolls and fraternal organization records. The Eureka County Recorder has mining claims and deeds from 1873. The Eureka Sentinel Museum in the original newspaper building preserves local history. For pre-1873 records of this area, check Lander County (Austin). The Eureka and Palisade Railroad records document employees who lived in the county. FamilySearch has microfilmed vital records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: No online access; county recorder in Eureka handles in-person requests

source_profiles.source_name: Eureka County Recorder — Land and Mining Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deeds and mining claims from county formation. Heavy mining claim volume from 1873–1900.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete — courthouse survived town fires of 1879 and 1880.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: One of Nevada's most important historical newspapers. Mining news, legal notices, obituaries, community events, labor news. Extensively digitized.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent digitization — nearly complete run available online.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from county formation. Note Italian and Cornish surnames in mining-era records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — continuous from 1873.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: First enumerated as Eureka County in 1880 (peak boom). Population dropped dramatically after 1890. 1870 data under Lander County.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete for surviving years.

source_profiles.source_name: Eureka County Court Records — Charcoal Burners' War Cases
source_profiles.repository: Nevada State Archives / Eureka County Courthouse
source_profiles.coverage_notes: District court records including the 1879 Charcoal Burners' War cases, mining dispute litigation, and probate matters.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — older records at state archives.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major record losses. Territorial-era records may be sparse for remote areas.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of Nevada's original nine counties created in 1861. Named for the Humboldt River (after Alexander von Humboldt). Winnemucca became the county seat, a key stop on the Central Pacific Railroad. Major Basque immigrant community. Ranching and mining (Orovada, Paradise Valley, Golconda) drove settlement. Pershing County carved out in 1919.
county_metadata.research_tips: Humboldt County is second only to Elko for Basque genealogical research in Nevada — Winnemucca had numerous Basque boarding houses (ostatuak) that served as community hubs, and their records, where they survive, are invaluable for tracing immigrant chains from the Pyrenees. The Center for Basque Studies at UNR has oral histories, boarding house registers, and family files. The Humboldt County Recorder in Winnemucca has deeds from the territorial era. The Humboldt Museum in Winnemucca preserves local family histories and photographs. Chinese workers on the Central Pacific Railroad and in local mines left records in the Chinese Exclusion Act files at NARA San Bruno. Paradise Valley was a significant ranching community and its school and church records survive at the Nevada Historical Society. Pershing County was carved from Humboldt in 1919 — pre-1919 records for that area (Lovelock, Imlay) are in Humboldt County files. FamilySearch has microfilmed county vital records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County recorder deed index available; online access limited, most records require in-person visit

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deeds and mining claims from territorial era. Includes records for Pershing County area before 1919.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete — no losses.

source_profiles.source_name: Basque Boarding House and Community Records — Winnemucca
source_profiles.repository: Center for Basque Studies, UNR / Nevada Historical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Boarding house registers, mutual aid society records, and oral histories from the Winnemucca Basque community. Documents immigration chains from Basque Country.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Scattered — some boarding house records survive; oral histories collected by UNR.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from territorial era. Note Basque and Chinese surnames in 19th-century records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — continuous from early settlement.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census 1870–1950 (1890 destroyed). Includes Pershing County area through 1910 census.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete for surviving years.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, estates, guardianships from territorial era. Includes pre-1919 Pershing County area probate.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well preserved from territorial era onward.

source_profiles.source_name: Silver State (Winnemucca) and Humboldt County Newspapers
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Winnemucca newspapers with railroad, ranching, and mining news. Basque community events. Legal notices and obituaries.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial digitization; microfilm at Nevada State Library.
